entry.title: Agentic Memory Unpacked: Families, Types, Architectures, and Lifecycle
entry.content:
In my previous article, I gave an overview of agentic memory — what it is, when to use it, and how it relates to learning. This article delves deeper into memory families, content types, organizational architectures, and the memory lifecycle. After reading it, you will be equipped with all the knowledge and vocabulary necessary to start your own memory experiments and step up as a competent discussion partner on the matter.
 
This article draws heavily on Memory in the Age of AI Agents: A survey, augmented by my own thoughts and examples. Memory in the Age of AI Agents is a great (and very long) read that paints a comprehensive picture of agentic memory research. This post can't substitute the full paper, but I hope it provides a practical starting point — and I invite you to adopt its taxonomies and vocabulary.
 

Memory Families: Where and how do agents store memories?

 

Vanilla LLMs are stateless across separate interactions. This observation serves as the basis for all discussions of memory. However, it is important to note that this does not mean that LLM cannot store knowledge. In fact, everything an LLM "knows" is encoded in its model weights. It is thus logical to consider continuous adaptation of model weights during runtime to store memories and insights on user and environment interactions. Because this strategy encodes memory information in model parameters, it is also known as parametric memory.
 
Another form of memory that lives close to the core of the LLM is latent memory. Latent memory is formed not in the model weights, but in its hidden states and caches that can be snapshotted, persisted, and loaded back into the next session. Both parametric and latent memory are advanced concepts that require deeper model understanding; future posts will cover them.

Finally, token-level memory refers to memory created and persisted in various forms outside the LLM, which is then injected into the context window of a new session as tokens.
 

 Token-level, Parametric, and Latent Memory:  Description and Analogy
Memory FamilyDescriptionHuman Analogy
Parametric memoryKnowledge encoded directly in the model's weights through training or fine-tuning. For example, a model fine-tuned on company-specific terminology and workflowsLearned and deeply internalized skills like riding a bike — you don't consult a manual, you know
Latent memoryInternal representations, such as hidden states or caches that can be persisted and restored. For example, a snapshotted KV cache from a prior session or compressed hidden state checkpoints.The "mental context" you hold while working on a problem — lose focus, and it's gone
Token-level memory
Explicit, discrete memory stored externally and injected as tokens into the LLM context window. Examples include conversation logs, user preference files, shared documents, and knowledge snippets retrieved from dedicated storage structures.
A notebook you carry to note down and develop thoughts and observations



Due to the nature of these memory families, they come with some inherent pros and cons:


Token-level, Parametric, and Latent Memory:  Comparison 
PropertyToken-level MemoryParametric & Latent Memory
AuditabilityAuditable, potentially human-readable, symbolicNon-auditable, non-human-readable
ExplainabilityHigh — memories can be inspected and understood directlyLow — individual updates are only meaningful within the context of all other weights
EfficiencyRetrieval overhead and increased latency during inferencePrincipally higher efficiency; no retrieval step needed
ReliabilityAdditional RAG link in the chain makes end-to-end pipeline potentially less reliableKnowledge is embedded in the model itself; similar reliability to world facts in LLM
Measurability
Full scan and inspection of stored knowledge is possibleCan only be measured by probing; no meaningful full scan
Natural fit
Tasks that need auditing, high-level strategies (type 2 thinking - deliberate, analytical)Fuzzy tasks like image recognition and control; tasks that escape explicit rule sets (type 1 thinking — fast, intuitive)

 

The remainder of this article focuses exclusively on the most intuitive and actively discussed memory family: token-level memory.
 

Memory Contents: What do we store?

 

My previous article discussed basic considerations regarding agentic memory use cases, but knowing when to use agentic memory is one thing; knowing what to store is another.

To delineate which types of content can be stored in agentic memory, I find it helpful to look at memory models. Drawing on a background of psychology and neuroscience, Endel Tulving proposed a memory model originally intended to describe human memory. This model is now often referenced in AI. Tulving's model distinguishes "semantic", "procedural", and "episodic memory". A slightly different but overlapping model is put forward in Memory in the Age of AI Agents. In it, "factual memory", "experiential memory", and "working memory" are distinguished.

 
Memory Models: Types, Subtypes, Description, and Examples
Lifespan & Memory TypeTulving EquivalentSubtypeDescriptionExample
Long-term:
Factual		SemanticUser factualFacts and preferences about the user."The user prefers concise answers."
  Environment factualFacts about the environment, domain, or world the agent operates in."The production database uses PostgreSQL 15."
Long-term:
Experiential		Procedural + EpisodicCase-basedMinimally processed records of historical episodes. Retain specific context and outcomes.A full interaction log of a debugging session that resolved a timeout error.
  Strategy-basedDistilled, transferable reasoning patterns, workflows, and high-level insights from past trajectories. Decouple decision-making from specific contexts."When facing erratic tests, first check for shared state between test cases before investigating timing issues."
  Skills-basedExecutable procedural capacities like atomic code snippets or other artifacts that operationalize abstract strategies.A reusable shell script for rotating log files, derived from repeated manual operations.
Short-term: Working——Capacity-limited, dynamically controlled collection of information for active context management during a single task or session. Erased after the session ends. The current conversation history, to-do lists, or scratchpad notes.
 

Notice that no strict boundaries exist between these types: memory architectures may or may not physically separate them, and one type can eventually be derived from the other. For example, information stored in working and episodic memory may be abstracted and condensed to form long-term semantic or procedural memories.
 

Information Architectures: How are memories organized?

 

Independent of the type of individual memories, they may be related; like a cluster of user preferences for text formatting (semantic), a set of events with a temporal history (episodic), or a bunch of code snippets for interacting with an external data source (procedural). The recent popularity of knowledge graphs is a testament to the importance of such relations in many agentic applications, prompting us to consider how memories may be connected.

The following are prototypic forms of organizations that an agentic memory corpus may have. In my opinion, it is helpful to distinguish and name them, because they are the perfect bridge between what we expect the memory to do and how we implement it structurally:
  • Flat Memory (1D): No explicit inter-unit topology. Memories are accumulated as sequences or bags of units (e.g., text snippets, trajectories, chunks). They may reference one another implicitly, e.g., "as known from a recent conversation".
  • Planar Memory (2D): A structured but single-layer organization within one plane: units are related by a graph, tree, table, and so on, with no cross-layer relations. The structure is explicit, but not layered (e.g., a single knowledge graph).
  • Hierarchical Memory (3D): Structured across multiple layers with inter-layer links, forming a volumetric or stratified memory. (e.g., multiple knowledge graphs at different levels of abstraction for layered retrieval.)

Just a few days ago, Andrej Karpathy shared his view on the importance of relations among agentic memories under the concept of an "LLM Wiki". Irrespective of whether we call this dynamic knowledge base a wiki or a memory corpus, the importance of cross-referencing in many practical applications is clear, as is the goal of creating and maintaining "a persistent, compounding artifact" of connected information.
 

Memory Lifecycle: How does it all come together? - A cradle-to-grave memory perspective

 

Bearing potential content and organizational structures in mind greatly helps articulate the purpose of an agentic memory system and begin drafting an implementation architecture. There is, however, another perspective that influences design: the memory lifecycle — from creation through alteration to eventual deletion.

The following are the three main lifecycle phases of agentic memory:
  • Formation – Capturing novel insights from interactions and experiences.
  • Retrieval – Intelligently surfacing relevant information to enhance the agent's context and decision-making for future tasks.
  • Evolution – Refining, synthesizing, and connecting stored knowledge through reflection.


Memory Formation "How to extract the memory (from traces)?"

Agentic memory forms from observable artifacts — agent logs and traces — but memories are typically more refined than raw logs. Forming them involves processing steps like the following:
  • Semantic Summarization is a lossy compression mechanism, distilling the gist of lengthy interaction logs. It does not interpret or refine the contained information other than by reducing noise and compressing it as is. Semantic summarization reduces context length, allowing more core insights to be loaded into a single LLM context window upon retrieval. Also, this form of summarization may help LLM focus on important parts of a log.
  • Knowledge Distillation aims to extract higher-order reusable knowledge from interaction trajectories and artifacts. This may involve relating a current episode to prior learning signals and records to spot a pattern carrying reusable knowledge, e.g., "this is the 10th time I am being asked to refactor a result to include docstrings, I will assume that the user always wants docstrings until further notice."
  • Structured Construction builds on top of memory units produced by semantic summarization and knowledge distillation. Structured construction is used to relate pieces of information to one another, i.e., to build a higher-order organization from flat memory units.

These processes often co-occur: processing a single interaction log might involve summarizing it, distilling a skill from recurring patterns, and linking that skill to related context descriptions for future retrieval.
 

Memory Retrieval "How to utilize memory?"

Agentic memory ultimately has to serve its purpose during runtime. At some point, an agent will be asked to provide help with a task that benefits from information contained in the memory corpus. Memory retrieval is fundamentally a RAG problem — the same metrics (accuracy, speed, cost) and techniques apply, just drawing from a memory-specific corpus. It is thus not a focus of this article. However, retrieval signals (e.g., frequency) can feed back into the evolution of memory, as you will see below.
 

Memory Evolution "How to refine the memory?"

Forming memories from agent traces provides the memory base with new insights. However, these insights may contradict, add to, or relate to prior memories. This is why long-running agentic memory solutions will inevitably need to offer ways to evolve memories over time, possibly even outside of active user sessions:
 

Memory Evolution Mechanisms: Purpose and Key Challenges
MechanismPurposeKey Challenge
ConsolidationMerge new and existing memories into generalized insights, ensuring cumulative learningDetermining the right granularity for matching and merging
UpdatingResolve conflicts between new and existing memories to maintain accuracy and relevanceStability-plasticity dilemma: when to overwrite vs. treat as noise
ForgettingRemove outdated or redundant information to maintain focus and efficiencyOverly aggressive pruning risks erasing rare but essential knowledge
 
 
In principle, humans can supply feedback or support throughout any of these lifecycle phases. E.g., humans can inspect the memory database and mark memories as outdated, update memories, or merge memories. This behavior is already implemented in solutions like ChatGPT's memory. In addition to manual and LLM work, deterministic rules can be used to evolve the memory base. E.g., memory forgetting can be modeled using deterministic measures such as time since creation or retrieval frequency.


What's Next?

 

This article, alongside its predecessor, serves as an introduction to agentic memory. Upcoming articles on the matter will focus on deep dives into topics like latent memory. With them, we are moving closer to the cutting edge of research and industry.
 
 
# CTO Research and Innovation Munich
@ladenaw

entry.title: SPROXY shows “Service outdated” only for newly created Integration Suite Service Interfaces
entry.content:
Hi everyone,

we are currently testing ABAP proxy generation with SAP Integration Suite.

We observed the following behavior in transaction SPROXY:

  • Migrated PI/PO service interfaces can be activated and do not show this issue
  • New service interfaces created directly in SAP Integration Suite can also be used to generate ABAP proxies
  • Runtime communication works: the ABAP proxy can receive messages from Integration Suite
  • However, in SPROXY these newly created interfaces remain in status “Service outdated”
  • Regeneration and activation of the proxy do not remove the status

 

So the issue seems to occur only for service interfaces modeled natively in Integration Suite, not for interfaces migrated from PI/PO.

Questions:

  1. Is this a known limitation or bug of ABAP proxy generation with newly created Integration Suite service interfaces?
  2. Can the “Service outdated” status be ignored if runtime communication is already working?
  3. Has anyone managed to get rid of this status for newly created interfaces?

Any experience would be greatly appreciated.

KR

Stefan

entry.title: Business Objects Enterprise 4.3 update from SP04 patch 8 to SP5 patch 4 failed !
entry.content:
Hello guys
I'm having big problems trying to upgrade an SAP Business Objects Enterprise 4.3 system, from SP4 patch 8 to SP05 patch 4.
First of all, I report the data of the system:
- SAP Business Objects Enterprise 4.3 SP04 Patch 08 single node.
- The web application server is Apache Tomcat 9.0.91.
- The CMS database is a locally installed SQL Anywhere 17 (17.11.7458).
- Everything is hosted by a Windows Server 2022 version 21H2 VM (OS Build 20348.4773).
- As CPU, the VM has 8 sockets and 16 virtual processors.
- The RAM amounts to 128GB.
The upgrade is necessary to close some security flaws found in the current version of the product (SAP notes 3654236, 3695912, 3697256).
I tried, for the first time, this upgrade, in mid-March and the activity ended "encountering errors".

The errors reported in the logs (ErrorsAndWarnings.log files) were all related to the impossibility of copying or moving files, for example:

Errors/Warnings found during the installation are as follows

17:45:48.198 Error: Could not move temp dest folder to dest folder.
17:45:48.198 Error: Temp dest folder: D:\SAP BusinessObjects\InstallData\tmp\monitoring.sdk.java-4.0-core-64
17:45:48.198 Error: Dest folder: D:\SAP BusinessObjects\InstallData\InstallCache\monitoring.sdk.java-4.0-core-64\14.3.5.5591\
17:45:48.198 Error: Class name: class Poco::FileAccessDeniedException
17:45:48.202 Error: Display text: Access to file denied: \\?\D:\SAP BusinessObjects\InstallData\tmp\monitoring.sdk.java-4.0-core-64
17:45:48.202 Error: Could not delete temp dest folder: D:\SAP BusinessObjects\InstallData\tmp\monitoring.sdk.java-4.0-core-64\
17:45:48.202 Error: Class name: class Poco::FileAccessDeniedException
17:45:48.202 Error: Display text: Access to file denied: \\?\D:\SAP BusinessObjects\InstallData\tmp\monitoring.sdk.java-4.0-core-64\actions\strings\ar.uitext.xml

(There are about 124 log lines with errors of this type).

I opened a SAP case about that and, the responsible for the problem, was identified in the antivirus and EDR software Microsoft Defender and CrowdStrike.
Then, I tried the upgrade again, after uninstalling CrowdStike and having some folders put in the Windows Defender’s antivirus exclusion list (the folder where SAP Business Objects is installed, and the folder where the update packages are stored).

As always, I followed all the directions in the SAP note 1952120 before I started.
I carried out the task, executing the upgrade exe file "as administrator" with a user that is member of the Administrators group of the machine, which is also the same user with which the SIA service is set.
Before starting, there was 153GB of free space on the drive where SBOP is installed and 66.2GB of free space on the system C: drive.
I made a first attempt to upgrade, to version 4.3 SP05 patch 4.
After a few minutes from the beginning of the procedure, I saw the file:
ErrorsAndWarnings.log quickly become 3KB large.
The activity then continued and, an hour and twelve minutes later, the upgrade ended with the message:
“SAP Business Objects BI platform 4.3 SP5 Patch 4 has encountered errors during the install process”.

The ErrorsAndWarnings.log log file contained virtually the same type of errors as the first upgrade attempt.

At that point, thinking that there might be something wrong with the upgrade packages, I reverted the virtual machine to the state before the upgrade (so system to version 4.3 SP04 patch 8 fully functional) and I tried a new upgrade to version 4.3 SP05 patch 5 (upgrade package released a few days ago).
Unfortunately, although the file ErrorsAndWarnings.log had far fewer errors, the upgrade was still finished, in an hour and fourteen minutes, always with the message:
“SAP Business Objects BI platform 4.3 SP5 Patch 5 has encountered errors during the install process”.

The ErrorsAndWarnings.log log file has the following content:

Errors/Warnings found during the installation are as follows

22:22:54.539 Error: Could not move temp dest folder to dest folder.
22:22:54.539 Error: Temp dest folder: D:\SAP BusinessObjects\InstallData\tmp\crystalreports.cpp.exporting.u2frdef-4.0-fr-64
22:22:54.539 Error: Dest folder: D:\SAP BusinessObjects\InstallData\InstallCache\crystalreports.cpp.exporting.u2frdef-4.0-fr-64\14.3.5.5657\
22:22:54.539 Error: Class name: class Poco::FileAccessDeniedException
22:22:54.539 Error: Display text: Access to file denied: \\?\D:\SAP BusinessObjects\InstallData\tmp\crystalreports.cpp.exporting.u2frdef-4.0-fr-64

However, I would like to point out that, although the upgrade ended with errors, the system seemed to be working correctly: all the processing servers were up & running and even the test of opening a report and its refresh did not give me problems.

I preferred, however, to revert the virtual machine and deliver the working system to the customer, albeit to the old version.

Do you have any idea what it may depend on?
I opened a new case to SAP support, but, in the meantime maybe somebody may help me.

Thank you very much.
Andrea

entry.title: Re: Practice System is full right now, but no worries......
entry.content:
Dear @verorach_97,

SAP does not publish a fixed wait time for that message. The only official timing SAP gives is that, after successful enrollment, some systems are available immediately and others may take up to 90 minutes to prepare but that is a different situation from a system being full

More details below:

https://learning.sap.com/helpcenter/learninghub-subscription/practice-systems

What to do:

Check whether you already have two active practice-system enrollments because SAP allows only two simultaneous course enrollments. If the issue continues raise a support case and select Practice Systems as the service category SAP states that this support team is available 24/5

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: Reuse a DTV (Data Transition Validation) Project in a New BTC Cycle
entry.content:
Overview

When you create a new cycle in the Manage Cycles app, the system automatically creates a new DTV project. In this situation, you may want to reuse the settings from an existing project.

Prerequisites

You created a new cycle in the Manage Cycles app and set the Run Data Transition Validation (DTV) Tool option to Yes.

Procedure

  1. In transaction DTV, export the project that has the settings you want to reuse.

  2. The system automatically creates a new DTV project when you create the cycle. In transaction DTV, locate this project and copy the project ID. This ID will be required in step 4.

    Note: The project ID is the same as the cycle ID that you can view in the Manage Cycles app.

  3. Change the project ID of the newly created project to a temporary name.

  4. Restart transaction DTV and import the project you exported in step 1, but specify the project ID you copied in step 2. This creates a new project with the project ID of your new cycle and that contains the configuration settings you want to reuse.

  5. (Optional) Delete the project you renamed in step 3.

entry.title: Re: Table names and string translation
entry.content:
Do You mean the second option with the stored procedure "TmSp_GetDefaultElementsforCR"?

No, you don't need any subreports. You only need to add the stored procedure "TmSp_GetDefaultElementsforCR" to the layout without linking it.

This procedure has two parameters: DocKey@ and ObjectId@. Therefore, the result of this procedure will be a single row with many columns (one column per text entry). The row contains the translations in the language of the document.

Here is the corresponding SAP note, where all of this is described:

https://me.sap.com/notes/2785541

This option (with the stored procedure "TmSp_GetDefaultElementsforCR") should be selected if the client requires translations in multiple languages. However, if only German and English are sufficient, I would recommend choosing the first option with variables for each text entry for performance reasons.

entry.title: 001 The "IDEA" in the Room
entry.content:
Two-thirds of business leaders say they don't fully trust their data. For organizations trying to deploy AI into critical workflows, that's not a statistic — it's a structural problem. The people closest to it — the data leaders, the architects, the CDOs navigating it daily — knew this long before the analysts did. And yet, until recently, nobody had built an institution to solve it.

This is the story of how one got started. And how you can get involved.

---

With an operational mind and background in accounting, I'm wired to see problems in terms of processes — where the money flows, where the logic breaks, where the gap between the model and the reality quietly widens until someone has to explain it to a board.

I grew up in Vancouver, built a career at SAP, and oddly I've spent more than a healthy amount of time thinking about institutions. The ones that exist because a problem was real enough, and persistent enough, that the people living inside it eventually decided to stop waiting for someone else to fix it.

That instinct had been with me for a long time. It took a dinner on a rainy Tuesday night in Vancouver to finally do something about it.

---

The first iteration surfaced around 2017. I was working inside SAP's New Ventures and Technology group — a genuinely progressive initiative that gave people like me the room to think commercially, take real risks, and build something with teeth. Along with David Brockington, I co-founded ADAZA by SAP there. Innovative work, interesting problems, and a front-row seat to the Canadian startup ecosystem as I began to build relationships with local accelerators.

What I saw was a talent community that was genuinely impressive in capability but often narrowly focused in ambition. A lot of energy going toward consumer apps and niche digital tools, while industries with massive, expensive, structurally complex problems sat a few hundred kilometers away. Forestry. Mining. Energy. Canada's economic backbone. The kind of industries where a well-aimed technology intervention doesn't just improve a conversion rate, it changes the economics of an entire operation.

The concept I kept returning to was a bridge. A proper institute — independent, practitioner-led — connecting the people who knew how to build with the industries that had something worth building for. Not a conference. Not a vendor program. A place where ideas met problems, and both sides left smarter.

It didn't happen. The timing wasn't right, the path wasn't clear, and I wasn't ready. But the shape of the idea stayed with me.

---

Fast forward to 2024. I'd spent the better part of six years leading operations for SAP's Business Technology Platform (BTP) marketing group, before becoming Chief of Staff to our CMO. Consequential work, but I'd been orchestrating someone else's programs for long enough. I wanted to own something again. Build something. Create direct impact, with a clear line from effort to outcome.

The vision of a think tank returned. This time, it had found the right problem.

I'd been watching the same conversation play out, over and over, across the data and AI landscape. Intelligent, experienced leaders — the people responsible for some of the most complex data environments on the planet — all carrying the same quiet frustration. Years of investment. Sophisticated platforms. Governance committees. And still, when it came time to actually trust the data underneath a consequential decision, there was a pause. A hesitation. A quiet but persistent gap between what the architecture was supposed to deliver and what the business actually experienced.

The numbers make the gap concrete. Nearly half of enterprises report active struggles with data fragmentation. Poor data quality costs the average organization $12.9 million per year — and that's the average. In a large, complex enterprise — a global bank, say, with decades of system accumulation, regulatory overlays, and acquired platforms that were never properly integrated — the figure is almost certainly higher, and largely untracked.

Meanwhile, AI had arrived at the party and was asking uncomfortable questions. Not about the technology — the models were impressive, the use cases were compelling. The problem was what sat underneath them. As one executive put it at a roundtable we later ran in Sydney: "98 percent of the world's data is not inside the large foundation models. It's locked in enterprises — in the systems." That's not a technology gap. That's a foundation problem.

Everyone was experimenting with AI. Copilots, generative tools, workflow automation — pilots were running in almost every organization we spoke with. But converting those experiments into scaled enterprise capability was proving elusive, particularly in regulated sectors where core business workflows depend on sensitive proprietary data, compliance requirements, and high-stakes decisions. Deploying AI into those environments requires something most organizations hadn't finished building: a data foundation they could actually trust.

The barrier wasn't the model. It was the meeting that had to happen before the model could be used. As another data leader at the same roundtable in Sydney put it rather directly: "The painful part for me is the governance around that… most of my time is going through all of the committees." Architecture review. Third-party risk. Responsible data governance bodies. Necessary, all of it — and collectively consuming the bandwidth of some of the sharpest people in the organization.

Scaling AI is less a technical challenge than a governance one. That line became a thesis.

---

What I couldn't find, in 2024, was a credible and independent space where senior leaders could sit down, be genuinely honest about this problem, and work through it together — across industries, across geographies, without a vendor agenda in the room. Analyst firms produce reports. Conferences produce panels. Vendors produce whitepapers. Nobody had built the institution.

Why does nobody own this?

That question had been sitting in the back of my mind for months when Dan Yu, our CMO for Data & Analytics, came to town.

Dan was in Vancouver for team meetings during what was, diplomatically, a period of organizational change. We carved out a Tuesday evening for dinner at Vij's, a local favourite just down the street. The kind of place where the food is good enough that you stop performing and just talk.

I didn't walk in with a deck. I walked in with a direction. A non-profit research organization dedicated to the advancement of data architecture and enterprise AI. Independent. Practitioner-led. Built on the insight that the problem wasn't technical — it was organizational, cultural, and structural. And that the only way to move the needle was to get the right people in a room, outside the commercial context, with enough trust to be honest about where things actually stood.

Dan had been part of establishing the TBM Council in a previous chapter of his career — a serious, respected organization that had done exactly this kind of work for technology business management. He understood the model. He understood what it took to build something that lasted. And he could see, as clearly as I could, that nothing like it existed for the data architecture conversation.

He didn't need much convincing.

We finished dinner. I walked home. And within a couple of weeks, I was writing up the plans.

---

There's a line from IDEA's first research brief that I keep coming back to: "The quality of your decisions will never exceed the quality of your data." Simple. Almost obvious. And yet most organizations are operating with a trust deficit they haven't fully priced. Companies that treat data as a strategic asset — not an IT problem — are 2.3 times more likely to grow revenue using AI. They make decisions 33% faster. They're not smarter. They've just fixed the foundation.

That was the gap IDEA was built to address. Not another vendor platform. Not another industry conference. An independent forum where the people responsible for enterprise data could work through the real problems together — and produce something the field could actually use.

What followed that dinner was the beginning of an experiment: could we build a genuinely independent space for the people responsible for the world's most complex data environments to solve this problem together? The roundtables, the research, the partnerships, the arguments about what the institution should and shouldn't be — that's what this is about.

---

*Next: Getting SAP to say yes — which, it turned out, was the easy part.*

---

**About IDEA**

IDEA — the Interchange for Data and Enterprise AI — is a programme of research, community building, and peer learning, delivered in partnership with the Open Data Institute (ODI). The ODI brings 14 years of experience stewarding independent, multi-stakeholder research across public and private sectors. Together, we're building a space where enterprise data leaders can cut through the noise, share what's actually working, and help shape a vendor-agnostic blueprint for AI-ready data infrastructure.

The programme is actively seeking involvement from data leaders, industry partners, and academic experts. If you work at the intersection of data, governance, and enterprise AI — and you have something real to contribute — we'd like to hear from you.

--> Find out more and get involved

entry.title: How to implement timeout for synchronous RFC calls from external systems (MuleSoft → SAPCAR)?
entry.content:
Hello SAP Experts,

We are calling SAPCAR from a MuleSoft API using synchronous RFC (SAP JCo).

Mule/JCo does not provide a way to enforce a real RFC execution response timeout. Once SAP starts processing, the RFC continues running on the SAP side and Mule keeps waiting for the response for long time.

What are the recommended SAP‑side options to control or limit the execution time of synchronous RFC calls?
Also Should long‑running SAPCAR logic avoid synchronous RFC and use async patterns instead?

Looking for SAP‑recommended, safe approaches to prevent long‑running RFCs.

Thanks in advance.

entry.title: Re: SAP Agentic AI in Practice: Concepts, Architecture, and Your First LangGraph Agent (Part 1 of 6)
entry.content:
@chetan_099 Thank you for your questions.

On the SDK package name — you are correct. generative-ai-hub-sdk is now deprecated and the replacement is pip install sap-ai-sdk-gen (SAP Cloud SDK for AI - Python). The good news is that class names are unchanged so all code in the series continues to work — only the installation command needs updating. I will revise the articles accordingly. Good catch.

On where to run the code and the LeanIX SAP AI agent hub question:

For the development environment, SAP BAS is the recommended option for interactive development and notebook-based exploration as covered in Part 2 of the Generative AI Hub series. For production, the agents in this series are designed to deploy on BTP Cloud Foundry as Flask or FastAPI applications.

On LeanIX — the SAP AI agent hub there is a governance and discovery layer, not an orchestration runtime. It helps organizations inventory and govern their AI agents across the enterprise. It doesn't replace LangChain or LangGraph — those still handle the reasoning, tool calling, and execution logic at runtime. Think of LeanIX as the control plane for agent governance and BTP as the runtime where agents actually execute. The two are complementary rather than competing, and your LangChain/LangGraph orchestration code is unaffected by it.

entry.title: How to traslate KTD documents for SAP BTP ABAP Environment?
entry.content:
Introduction

When you create a KTD document in SAP BTP ABAP Environment you will find out that there is no support for the translation of these documents.  

In this blog post I will describe a workaround that can be used until a standard solution will be provided by SAP.

Workaround

I managed to translate a KTD document “manually.”

  1. I logged in with German and created a KTD document.
  2. Then I exported the KTD document into a Git repository.
  3. Then I created a new entry in the XML for the language key “E” , chose a new UUID as the key, and entered the new text Base64 encoded (!). 
  4. Finally I imported the content of the Github repository again, thereby creating a translated version of the KTD document. 

<?xml version="1.0" encoding="utf-8"?>
<abapGit version="v1.0.0" serializer="LCL_OBJECT_SKTD" serializer_version="v1.0.0">
 <asx:abap xmlns:asx="http://www.sap.com/abapxml" version="1.0">
  <asx:values>
   <KTD_OBJECT>
    <NAME>ZZ10R_CART</NAME>
    <TYPE>GENERAL_TEXT</TYPE>
    <SOURCE_CONTENT_TYPE>02</SOURCE_CONTENT_TYPE>
    <REF_OBJ_TYPE>DDLS</REF_OBJ_TYPE>
    <REF_OBJ_NAME>ZZ10R_CART</REF_OBJ_NAME>
    <ABAP_LANGUAGE_VERSION>X</ABAP_LANGUAGE_VERSION>
    <CONTENT>
     <item>
      <NAME>ZZ10R_CART</NAME>
      <LANGU>D</LANGU>
      <COMPONENT_UUID>1rDFSNW0H9Cq1O2Gk1jBBg==</COMPONENT_UUID>
      <SOURCE_CONTENT>RG9rdSBpbiBEZXV0c2NoCgphbmQgbm93IGFkZCBzb21ldGhpbmcgbG9nZ2VkIG9uIGluIEVuZ2xp
c2g=</SOURCE_CONTENT>
     </item>
     <item>
      <NAME>ZZ10R_CART</NAME>
      <LANGU>E</LANGU>
      <COMPONENT_UUID>9u8rknFsH+Cq1DFMgPUzaA==</COMPONENT_UUID>
      <SOURCE_CONTENT>IyBub3cgb3JpZ25hbCBlbmdsaXNo</SOURCE_CONTENT>
     </item>
    </CONTENT>
   </KTD_OBJECT>
  </asx:values>
 </asx:abap>
</abapGit>

As a result, I see different texts when I am logged in with English or German.

Known issues

• If you edit a KTD document with ADT while being logged in in another language, you will “see” the version created in the other language. There is no error message.
• In such a case, you can even edit the KTD document and the changes are visible for both login languages.
• Only if, as described above, you export the KTD document via the “Github” detour and create a version in another language, will you have two texts.

entry.title: Re: Crossboarding process (internal hire process) should trigger from Recruitment Management Only
entry.content:
@NathanWilkinson many customers don't want that differentiation, sometimes they use same event reason for statistics, and don't want to differentiate between event reasons or just don't like to have an event reason name "this transfer triggers crossboarding" and "this transfer does not trigger crossboarding" there should be a way not forcing us to massive changes. 

What you mention is true, that value will remain and trigger crossboarding from EC.

I've been playing with process. processtrigger. process initiation type values in the select process variant. Either no variant for crossboarding from EC, or a variant with only start step, in this last case process hire status is hired and we can live with having these internal hires from EC in dashboard as far as we do nothing on them, however process.status remains open.

I guess we could always have a hidden field in job requisition, add it to mapping and use it to initiate crossboarding or not (similar to source of onboarding), but we have same issue, we need to be sure that field is always overwritten (oninit? ) but there are always be cases when those rules are not working (API)... 

Have you @NathanWilkinson found a way not using different event reasons? I don't think this should be so hard

 

Cheers,

Francisco

entry.title: From Copilot to Colleague: How SAP Is Shaping the Next Era of AI Driven Procurement
entry.content:
From Copilot to Colleague: How SAP Is Shaping the Next Era of AI‑Driven Procurement 🤖

Artificial intelligence has crossed a critical threshold in enterprise software. What began as experimentation with chat interfaces has rapidly evolved into a strategic conversation about how work itself is performed. In a recent discussion with Ryan Skorupski, Senior Director of AI/ML at SAP Ariba, the conversation explored how AI is moving from assistive tooling to embedded intelligence—and what that shift means for procurement leaders navigating increasing complexity, data volume, and speed expectations. 🚀

AI Didn’t Arrive Overnight—But the Moment Has Arrived 🌱

While generative AI may feel new, the technologies behind it—machine learning, language modeling, and predictive analytics—have been advancing in academia and research environments for more than a decade. What changed was accessibility. When large language models became available to a broader audience, leaders experienced firsthand how AI could reason across information, synthesize insights, and support decision‑making at scale.

For procurement, this marked a turning point. AI was no longer limited to narrow automation use cases; it became capable of understanding context, language, and intent. As Ryan noted, this moment forced organizations to rethink not just what they automate, but how procurement work should be designed in an AI‑first world. 💡

Beyond Chatbots: The Rise of the Procurement Copilot ✈️

Much of today’s AI adoption centers on copilots—context‑aware assistants embedded directly into enterprise applications. In SAP Ariba, copilots are designed to work alongside users, helping them navigate sourcing events, analyze data, and interact with complex workflows using natural language.

Unlike traditional chatbots, copilots are grounded in business context. They understand procurement data, permissions, and workflows, allowing them to assist with real tasks—not just answer questions. This represents a significant step forward in usability and productivity, particularly for teams balancing speed, compliance, and accuracy. 🔍

The Next Leap: Agentic AI in Procurement 🤝

While copilots enhance productivity, Ryan highlighted that the next major evolution is AI agents—systems capable of taking action, not just providing guidance. An AI agent can assemble an RFP, analyze historical sourcing events, recommend negotiation strategies, and prepare supplier communications with minimal human intervention.

Importantly, the near‑term goal is not perfection or full autonomy. The value today lies in time savings and scale. AI agents can handle large portions of repetitive, rules‑based work, while humans remain in the loop to review, adjust, and approve outcomes. Over time, as trust and accuracy improve, higher levels of autonomy become possible—especially for low‑risk, high‑volume categories. ⚖️

Why Data Foundations Matter More Than Models 🧠

A recurring theme in the discussion was that AI success depends less on algorithms and more on data quality and structure. Procurement data is often fragmented, inconsistently labeled, or repurposed in ways AI cannot easily interpret. AI cannot “clean” data without understanding its meaning.

This is where SAP’s investment in knowledge graphs becomes critical. By layering semantic meaning over enterprise data—defining what each field, object, and API represents in natural language—AI systems can reason about business data the same way people do. Knowledge graphs provide the connective tissue that allows AI to scale reliably across procurement, finance, and supply chain domains. 📊

Training AI Responsibly—Without Compromising Trust 🔐

One of the most common concerns from customers is how enterprise data is used by AI. Ryan clarified that SAP’s approach centers on pre‑trained models that leverage customer data at inference time, not to train shared public models.

Looking ahead, however, the greatest value will come from customer‑specific AI—models fine‑tuned to an organization’s processes, terminology, and data. This mirrors how employees are trained internally and enables AI to function as a true digital colleague. Strong governance, security, and transparency will remain essential as this capability evolves. 🧩

A New Interaction Model for Enterprise Work 👓

As AI matures, the way users interact with enterprise systems will change. Static dashboards may give way to dynamically generated views. Mobile and tablet‑based approvals will become more common. Over time, augmented and mixed‑reality interfaces may further reduce friction by delivering insights directly in context.

The unifying theme is abstraction: AI handles orchestration, data retrieval, and execution, while users focus on judgment, strategy, and outcomes. This shift has the potential to significantly reshape how procurement teams operate at scale. 🌐

The Future of Procurement Is Human‑Led, AI‑Powered 🎯

AI will not replace procurement professionals—but it will redefine their role. The future belongs to organizations that invest early in data foundations, AI literacy, and trust‑based adoption. As AI evolves from tool, to copilot, to agent, SAP’s focus remains clear: embed intelligence responsibly, keep humans in control, and deliver tangible business value.

The question is no longer whether AI will transform procurement—but how intentionally leaders choose to shape that transformation. 🔮

LISTEN HERE:

Apple:  https://podcasts.apple.com/us/podcast/episode-029-diving-deep-into-ai-in-procurement-with/id1764078646?i=1000760634770

Spotify:  Spotify:  https://open.spotify.com/episode/0DoASvDgt3tzox6D6KAmu6?si=QXxta4KzS6mO7YF2SS9MVQ

entry.title: AI authorship accusations - how do we do it exactly?
entry.content:
Hi All -

I am trying to publish a blog post about my new development for tracking operations with NFC checkpoints and its integration I did with SAP. However, my post is getting rejected because, I am citing:

This blog post is AI-generated. Purely AI-generated content is not the kind of content we are looking for in the SAP Community. Per our rules of engagement: "authors should share their persona l knowledge.

I am a bit baffled as to how to deal with it... I did use LLM to correct my grammar, but both first and final drafts were created/corrected by myself. More importantly, the development I am talking about is quite unique regardless the language. Besides, I do not see if there is a way to engage with the moderator: there is no way to reply to the feedback on "returned to author" state. Are there any criteria or tests I need to run my blog post through before submitting?

    На вас чекає: Автоматизація MRP та виробничих замовлень, динамічне планування та оптимізація операцій за допомогою AI.

entry.title: Human-in-the-Loop SAP Agents: Approval, Escalation, and Audit (Series 2, Part 5 of 6)
entry.content:
SAP COMMUNITY  |  SERIES 2: AGENTIC AI IN PRACTICE  |  PART 5 OF 6

Human-in-the-Loop SAP Agents: Approval, Escalation, and Audit

Five production patterns for keeping humans in control of agentic SAP systems — approval workflows using LangGraph interrupt(), confidence-based routing, smart escalation, timeout and fallback handling, and audit trail architecture — built on SAP migration and S/4HANA change request use cases.

By Deepa Dorairaj   |  SAP AI Solution Architect  |  Series 2, Part 5 of 6  |  Published 2026

TL;DR: Agentic AI systems that operate autonomously in SAP landscapes must have structured human checkpoints — not as a limitation, but as the architecture that makes them trustworthy enough to deploy in production. This article covers five human-in-the-loop patterns: approval workflows using LangGraph's interrupt() mechanism, confidence-based autonomous vs. escalation routing, smart escalation to the right person by exception type, timeout and fallback handling when humans don't respond, and audit trail architecture that satisfies enterprise compliance requirements. Two SAP use cases anchor the examples: migration sign-off approval from Part 4 and S/4HANA change request approval.

Series Context

This is Part 5 of the Agentic AI in Practice series. Part 4 established the principle — agents flag, humans decide. This part implements that principle in production code across five patterns with two real SAP use cases.

The patterns here apply to any agentic SAP workload — not just migration. Once you understand how LangGraph's interrupt mechanism works with Teams notifications and audit logging, you can apply it to procurement approvals, change request reviews, exception handling in any business process.

WHY HUMAN-IN-THE-LOOP IS AN ARCHITECTURE DECISION, NOT A FEATURE 

The Case for Structured Human Checkpoints

The temptation with agentic systems is to minimize human touchpoints — the less interruption, the more automation value. This is the wrong frame for SAP contexts.

SAP systems are systems of record. Actions taken in them — purchase orders raised, materials deactivated, migration objects loaded — have financial, operational, and compliance consequences that cannot be easily reversed. An agent that acts autonomously in these systems without structured human checkpoints is not more capable. It is more dangerous.

Human-in-the-loop is not about limiting what agents can do. It is about defining precisely which decisions require human judgment and ensuring those decisions always reach the right person with the right context at the right time.

Pattern

What It Does

SAP Example

Approval workflow

Agent pauses at a defined checkpoint and waits for explicit human approval before proceeding

Migration sign-off, change request authorization

Confidence-based routing

Agent decides autonomously when confidence is HIGH, escalates when MEDIUM or LOW

Data quality resolution, mapping ambiguity

Smart escalation

Agent routes to the correct person based on exception type and severity

Critical migration errors, compliance violations

Timeout and fallback

Agent handles non-response with defined fallback behavior

Approval delays during migration windows

Audit trail

Every agent decision is logged with inputs, reasoning, and outcome for compliance

All production agentic workloads

PATTERN 1: APPROVAL WORKFLOW WITH LANGGRAPH INTERRUPT() 

  ▶  LangGraph interrupt() — Agent Pauses for Human Approval 

Pattern 1: Approval Workflow

LangGraph's interrupt() function pauses graph execution at a defined node, persists the current state to a checkpointer, and waits for human input before resuming. This is the correct mechanism for approval workflows — the agent's work is preserved, the human reviews the agent's output and decision, and execution continues from exactly where it paused.

Agent completes work → interrupt() — pause → Human reviews → Approve / Reject → Resume or terminate
# Pattern 1: Approval workflow using LangGraph interrupt()
from langgraph.graph import StateGraph, END
from langgraph.checkpoint.memory import MemorySaver
from langgraph.types import interrupt, Command
from typing import TypedDict, Literal
 
class MigrationApprovalState(TypedDict):
    sign_off_report:   str
    sign_off_status:   str    # APPROVED | CONDITIONAL | REJECTED
    discrepancies:     list
    human_decision:    str    # 'approve' | 'reject' | 'conditional'
    human_comments:    str
    final_status:      str
    audit_log:         list
 
# Node: Agent prepares sign-off package for human review
def prepare_sign_off(state: MigrationApprovalState) -> MigrationApprovalState:
    # Agent has already generated sign-off report (from Part 4 Agent 5)
    # Log the preparation step
    state['audit_log'].append({
        'timestamp': datetime.now().isoformat(),
        'action': 'SIGN_OFF_PREPARED',
        'agent_recommendation': state['sign_off_status'],
        'discrepancy_count': len(state['discrepancies'])
    })
    return state
 
# Node: interrupt() pauses execution and waits for human decision
def request_human_approval(state: MigrationApprovalState) -> MigrationApprovalState:
    """
    interrupt() suspends the graph here.
    State is persisted to checkpointer.
    Execution resumes when resume() is called with human decision.
    """
    human_input = interrupt({
        'message': 'Migration sign-off requires your approval.',
        'agent_recommendation': state['sign_off_status'],
        'report_summary': state['sign_off_report'][:500],
        'discrepancies': state['discrepancies'],
        'options': ['approve', 'conditional', 'reject']
    })
    state['human_decision'] = human_input['decision']
    state['human_comments'] = human_input.get('comments', '')
    state['audit_log'].append({
        'timestamp': datetime.now().isoformat(),
        'action': 'HUMAN_DECISION',
        'decision': human_input['decision'],
        'reviewer': human_input.get('reviewer', 'unknown'),
        'comments': human_input.get('comments', '')
    })
    return state
 
# Node: process outcome based on human decision
def process_approval_decision(state: MigrationApprovalState) -> MigrationApprovalState:
    decision = state['human_decision']
    if decision == 'approve':
        state['final_status'] = 'MIGRATION_APPROVED'
    elif decision == 'conditional':
        state['final_status'] = 'MIGRATION_APPROVED_WITH_CONDITIONS'
    else:
        state['final_status'] = 'MIGRATION_REJECTED'
    state['audit_log'].append({
        'timestamp': datetime.now().isoformat(),
        'action': 'FINAL_STATUS_SET',
        'status': state['final_status']
    })
    return state
 
# Build graph with MemorySaver checkpointer — required for interrupt()
checkpointer = MemorySaver()
approval_graph = StateGraph(MigrationApprovalState)
approval_graph.add_node('prepare', prepare_sign_off)
approval_graph.add_node('request_approval', request_human_approval)
approval_graph.add_node('process_decision', process_approval_decision)
approval_graph.add_edge('prepare', 'request_approval')
approval_graph.add_edge('request_approval', 'process_decision')
approval_graph.add_edge('process_decision', END)
approval_graph.set_entry_point('prepare')
approval_agent = approval_graph.compile(checkpointer=checkpointer, interrupt_before=['request_approval'])
 
# Resume after human provides decision
def resume_with_human_decision(thread_id, decision, reviewer, comments=''):
    config = {'configurable': {'thread_id': thread_id}}
    approval_agent.invoke(
        Command(resume={'decision': decision, 'reviewer': reviewer, 'comments': comments}),
        config=confi

A checkpointer is mandatory for interrupt() to work. Without it, graph state is not persisted and the interrupt cannot be resumed. MemorySaver works for development and testing. For production, use a database-backed checkpointer (PostgreSQL or HANA Cloud) so approval state survives application restarts.

PATTERN 2: CONFIDENCE-BASED ROUTING 

  ▶  Agent decides autonomously when confident — escalates when not 

Pattern 2: Confidence-Based Autonomous vs. Escalation Routing

Not every agent decision requires human review. Confidence-based routing lets the agent act autonomously on HIGH confidence decisions while escalating MEDIUM and LOW confidence cases. This maximizes automation value while preserving human oversight where it actually matters.

Agent decision → Assess confidence → HIGH → autonomous → MEDIUM/LOW → escalate
# Pattern 2: Confidence-based routing for S/4HANA change request
class ChangeRequestState(TypedDict):
    change_request:    dict
    risk_assessment:   dict
    confidence:        str    # 'HIGH' | 'MEDIUM' | 'LOW'
    auto_approved:     bool
    escalation_target: str
    decision:          str
    audit_log:         list
 
def assess_change_risk(state: ChangeRequestState) -> ChangeRequestState:
    """Agent analyzes change request and assigns confidence level."""
    messages = [
        SystemMessage(content='''Assess this S/4HANA change request.
        Return JSON: {risk_level: LOW|MEDIUM|HIGH|CRITICAL,
        confidence: HIGH|MEDIUM|LOW,
        auto_approve_eligible: bool,
        reasoning: str,
        escalation_role: str}
        AUTO_APPROVE only if: LOW risk, HIGH confidence, no financial impact > 10k,
        no core customizing changes, standard transport route.'''),
        HumanMessage(content=f'Change request: {json.dumps(state["change_request"])}')
    ]
    result = json.loads(llm(messages).content)
    state['risk_assessment'] = result
    state['confidence'] = result['confidence']
    state['auto_approved'] = result['auto_approve_eligible'] and result['confidence'] == 'HIGH'
    state['escalation_target'] = result.get('escalation_role', 'change_manager')
    return state
 
# Conditional edge: route based on confidence and auto-approval eligibility
def route_by_confidence(state: ChangeRequestState) -> str:
    if state['auto_approved']:
        return 'auto_approve'
    elif state['confidence'] == 'MEDIUM':
        return 'request_review'    # Human review but not full approval flow
    else:
        return 'escalate'          # Full escalation to change manager

PATTERN 3: SMART ESCALATION 

  ▶  Route exceptions to the right person based on type and severity 

Pattern 3: Smart Escalation Routing

Not all exceptions should go to the same person. A financial variance in migration reconciliation goes to the Finance Lead. A technical ABAP error goes to the Basis team. A security-related change request goes to the GRC team. Smart escalation uses AI to classify exceptions and route them to the correct stakeholder — with full context, not just an alert.

# Pattern 3: Smart escalation with role-based routing
ESCALATION_MATRIX = {
    'FINANCIAL_VARIANCE':   {'role': 'finance_lead',    'sla_hours': 2},
    'TECHNICAL_ERROR':      {'role': 'basis_team',      'sla_hours': 4},
    'DATA_QUALITY':         {'role': 'data_owner',      'sla_hours': 8},
    'SECURITY_VIOLATION':   {'role': 'grc_team',        'sla_hours': 1},
    'COMPLIANCE_RISK':      {'role': 'compliance_lead', 'sla_hours': 2},
    'PERFORMANCE_ISSUE':    {'role': 'basis_team',      'sla_hours': 4},
}
 
def smart_escalate(state, exception_text: str, context: dict) -> dict:
    """Classify exception and route to correct stakeholder via Teams + Email."""
    # Step 1: Classify exception type
    messages = [
        SystemMessage(content=f'''Classify this SAP exception.
        Return JSON: {{exception_type, severity, affected_area, recommended_action}}
        Exception types: {list(ESCALATION_MATRIX.keys())}'''),
        HumanMessage(content=f'Exception: {exception_text}\nContext: {json.dumps(context)}')
    ]
    classification = json.loads(llm(messages).content)
    exception_type = classification['exception_type']
 
    # Step 2: Look up escalation target and SLA
    escalation = ESCALATION_MATRIX.get(exception_type,
                 {'role': 'change_manager', 'sla_hours': 4})
 
    # Step 3: Generate context-rich escalation message via Claude
    msg_response = claude_client.messages.create(
        model='claude-sonnet-4-20250514', max_tokens=400,
        messages=[{'role': 'user', 'content': f'''
Write a concise escalation notification for {escalation['role']}.
Exception: {exception_text}
Classification: {json.dumps(classification)}
SLA: {escalation['sla_hours']} hours. Include: what happened,
impact, recommended action, urgency. Professional tone.'''}]
    )
    escalation_message = msg_response.content[0].text
 
    # Step 4: Deliver via Teams and email
    recipient = get_role_contact(escalation['role'])
    send_teams_message(ms_token, recipient['email'], escalation_message)
    send_email(ms_token, recipient['email'],
               f"SAP Exception Escalation: {exception_type}", escalation_message)
 
    return {'type': exception_type, 'routed_to': escalation['role'],
            'sla_hours': escalation['sla_hours'], 'message': escalation_message}

PATTERN 4: TIMEOUT AND FALLBACK HANDLING 

  ▶  What happens when humans don't respond 

Pattern 4: Timeout and Fallback Handling

Migration windows are time-constrained. If an agent requests human approval at 2am and the approver doesn't respond within the SLA window, the agent needs a defined fallback behavior — not a silent hang. Three fallback strategies cover most SAP scenarios:

  • Escalate up — notify the next level in the approval chain
  • Safe halt — pause the migration, preserve state, alert all stakeholders
  • Proceed with logging — continue autonomously for LOW risk decisions, log prominently
# Pattern 4: Timeout handling with fallback strategies
import asyncio
from datetime import datetime, timedelta
 
async def request_approval_with_timeout(
    approval_request: dict,
    timeout_hours: float = 2.0,
    fallback_strategy: str = 'escalate_up'  # 'escalate_up'|'safe_halt'|'proceed'
) -> dict:
    """
    Request human approval with configurable timeout and fallback.
    Sends initial notification then polls for response.
    """
    deadline = datetime.now() + timedelta(hours=timeout_hours)
    thread_id = approval_request['thread_id']
 
    # Send initial approval request
    await send_approval_notification(approval_request)
 
    # Poll for response until deadline
    while datetime.now() < deadline:
        response = check_approval_response(thread_id)
        if response:
            return {'status': 'RESPONDED', 'decision': response}
 
        # Send reminder at 50% of timeout window
        elapsed = (datetime.now() - (deadline - timedelta(hours=timeout_hours))).total_seconds()
        if elapsed > timeout_hours * 3600 * 0.5 and not approval_request.get('reminder_sent'):
            await send_reminder_notification(approval_request)
            approval_request['reminder_sent'] = True
 
        await asyncio.sleep(300)  # Poll every 5 minutes
 
    # Timeout reached — apply fallback strategy
    return await apply_fallback(approval_request, fallback_strategy)
 
async def apply_fallback(request: dict, strategy: str) -> dict:
    if strategy == 'escalate_up':
        # Notify next level in approval chain
        escalation_target = get_next_approver(request['requested_from'])
        await send_escalation_notification(request, escalation_target)
        return {'status': 'ESCALATED', 'escalated_to': escalation_target}
 
    elif strategy == 'safe_halt':
        # Halt migration, preserve state, alert all stakeholders
        await notify_all_stakeholders(request, 'MIGRATION_HALTED_APPROVAL_TIMEOUT')
        return {'status': 'HALTED', 'reason': 'APPROVAL_TIMEOUT'}
 
    elif strategy == 'proceed':
        # Proceed autonomously — only for explicitly LOW risk decisions
        log_autonomous_proceed(request)
        return {'status': 'PROCEEDED_AUTONOMOUSLY', 'risk_accepted': True}

The 'proceed' fallback strategy should only be configured for explicitly LOW risk, fully reversible decisions. Never use 'proceed' as a fallback for migration execution, financial postings, or any action that cannot be undone. Document every instance of autonomous proceed in the audit trail with the risk acceptance rationale.

PATTERN 5: AUDIT TRAIL ARCHITECTURE 

  ▶  Every agent decision logged for compliance 

Pattern 5: Audit Trail Architecture

Enterprise compliance requirements for agentic SAP systems are not the same as application logging. An audit trail must capture not just what happened but why — what the agent's inputs were, what reasoning it applied, what it decided, and what the human review outcome was. This is what auditors and compliance teams need to validate that AI-assisted SAP decisions were appropriate.

# Pattern 5: Comprehensive audit trail for agentic SAP decisions
from dataclasses import dataclass, asdict
from datetime import datetime
import hdbcli.dbapi as hdb
 
@dataclass
class AgentAuditEntry:
    entry_id:          str
    timestamp:         str
    session_id:        str    # Links all entries for one agent run
    agent_name:        str
    node_name:         str    # Which LangGraph node
    action_type:       str    # AUTONOMOUS | ESCALATED | APPROVED | REJECTED | TIMEOUT
    input_summary:     str    # Truncated inputs (no sensitive data)
    ai_reasoning:      str    # Model's reasoning for the decision
    ai_confidence:     str    # HIGH | MEDIUM | LOW
    decision:          str    # What was decided
    human_reviewer:    str    # Email if human was involved
    human_decision:    str    # Human's decision if applicable
    human_comments:    str
    sap_objects:       str    # Affected SAP objects (JSON)
    compliance_flags:  str    # Any compliance considerations flagged
 
class AgentAuditLogger:
    def __init__(self, hana_conn):
        self.conn = hana_conn
        self._ensure_audit_table()
 
    def _ensure_audit_table(self):
        self.conn.cursor().execute('''
            CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS AI_AUDIT.AGENT_DECISIONS (
                ENTRY_ID        NVARCHAR(100) PRIMARY KEY,
                TIMESTAMP       TIMESTAMP NOT NULL,
                SESSION_ID      NVARCHAR(100) NOT NULL,
                AGENT_NAME      NVARCHAR(100),
                NODE_NAME       NVARCHAR(100),
                ACTION_TYPE     NVARCHAR(50),
                INPUT_SUMMARY   NCLOB,
                AI_REASONING    NCLOB,
                AI_CONFIDENCE   NVARCHAR(20),
                DECISION        NCLOB,
                HUMAN_REVIEWER  NVARCHAR(200),
                HUMAN_DECISION  NVARCHAR(100),
                HUMAN_COMMENTS  NCLOB,
                SAP_OBJECTS     NCLOB,
                COMPLIANCE_FLAGS NCLOB
            )
        ''')
        self.conn.commit()
 
    def log(self, entry: AgentAuditEntry):
        cursor = self.conn.cursor()
        cursor.execute('''
            INSERT INTO AI_AUDIT.AGENT_DECISIONS VALUES
            (?,?,?,?,?,?,?,?,?,?,?,?,?,?,?)
        ''', list(asdict(entry).values()))
        self.conn.commit()
 
    def get_session_trail(self, session_id: str) -> list:
        """Retrieve complete audit trail for one agent run — for compliance review."""
        cursor = self.conn.cursor()
        cursor.execute('''
            SELECT * FROM AI_AUDIT.AGENT_DECISIONS
            WHERE SESSION_ID = ?
            ORDER BY TIMESTAMP ASC
        ''', [session_id])
        return cursor.fetchall()

Store audit entries in HANA Cloud — the same instance used for the vector store in Part 4. This keeps all AI-related data in one governed location with the same access controls and backup policies as your SAP operational data. Audit entries should be treated as immutable — no updates, no deletes. Append only.

MICROSOFT TEAMS INTEGRATION FOR APPROVAL NOTIFICATIONS 

Delivering Approval Requests via Microsoft Teams

The interrupt() pattern pauses the agent, but humans still need to be notified and need a way to respond. Microsoft Graph API delivers the approval request as a Teams message and captures the response. This connects the LangGraph approval flow to the communication channel SAP practitioners already use.

# Teams notification for approval request
def send_approval_request_teams(ms_token: str, approver_email: str,
                                approval_data: dict, thread_id: str):
    """
    Send structured approval request to approver via Teams.
    Includes agent recommendation, key data, and response instructions.
    """
    message_body = f'''
SAP Migration Sign-Off Request
================================
Agent Recommendation: {approval_data['agent_recommendation']}
Discrepancies Found: {approval_data['discrepancy_count']}
Migration Objects: {', '.join(approval_data['migration_objects'])}
 
Summary:
{approval_data['report_summary']}
 
To respond, reply to this message with:
APPROVE - to approve migration sign-off
CONDITIONAL: [your conditions] - to approve with conditions
REJECT: [reason] - to reject
 
Thread ID: {thread_id} (required in your response)
Response required within 2 hours.
    '''
    send_teams_message(ms_token, approver_email, message_body,
                       subject='Action Required: SAP Migration Sign-Off')

COMPLETE HUMAN-IN-THE-LOOP FLOW 

S/4HANA Change Request: Complete Human-in-the-Loop Flow

This example combines all five patterns — confidence routing, smart escalation, approval workflow, timeout handling, and audit trail — into a single change request processing flow:

CR received → Assess risk + confidence → HIGH: auto-approve → LOW/MEDIUM: request approval → Timeout? Escalate up → Audit all steps
# Complete HITL flow for S/4HANA change request processing
async def process_change_request_with_hitl(change_request: dict) -> dict:
    session_id = str(uuid.uuid4())
    audit_logger = AgentAuditLogger(hana_conn)
 
    # Step 1: Assess risk and confidence
    assessment = assess_change_risk({'change_request': change_request, ...})
    audit_logger.log(AgentAuditEntry(
        entry_id=str(uuid.uuid4()), session_id=session_id,
        agent_name='ChangeRequestAgent', node_name='assess_risk',
        action_type='AUTONOMOUS',
        ai_reasoning=assessment['risk_assessment']['reasoning'],
        ai_confidence=assessment['confidence'],
        decision=f"Risk: {assessment['risk_assessment']['risk_level']}",
        ...
    ))
 
    # Step 2: Route based on confidence
    if assessment['auto_approved']:
        audit_logger.log(AgentAuditEntry(action_type='AUTONOMOUS', ...))
        return {'status': 'AUTO_APPROVED', 'session_id': session_id}
 
    # Step 3: Request human approval with timeout
    approval_result = await request_approval_with_timeout(
        approval_request={
            'thread_id': session_id,
            'requested_from': assessment['escalation_target'],
            'change_request': change_request,
            'risk_assessment': assessment['risk_assessment']
        },
        timeout_hours=2.0,
        fallback_strategy='escalate_up'
    )
 
    # Step 4: Log final outcome
    audit_logger.log(AgentAuditEntry(
        action_type=approval_result['status'],
        human_reviewer=approval_result.get('reviewer', ''),
        human_decision=approval_result.get('decision', ''),
        ...
    ))
 
    return {'status': approval_result['status'], 'session_id': session_id}

PRODUCTION DESIGN PRINCIPLES 

Human-in-the-Loop Design Principles for SAP Agents

Principle

Implementation Guidance

Define human checkpoints before writing code

Identify which decisions require human oversight before building the agent. Adding checkpoints retroactively is significantly harder than designing them in from the start.

Use database checkpointers in production

MemorySaver loses state on restart. Production approval workflows must survive application restarts — use PostgreSQL or HANA Cloud-backed checkpointers.

Never use 'proceed' fallback for irreversible actions

Autonomous proceed is only acceptable for LOW risk, fully reversible decisions. Document every instance explicitly in the audit trail with risk acceptance rationale.

Audit trail is not optional

Every agentic decision in a production SAP system needs an auditable record. Compliance teams will ask for it. Build it from day one, not as an afterthought.

SLA-driven timeout escalation

Every approval request needs a timeout and fallback. Define SLAs by exception type — financial variances get 2 hours, data quality issues get 8. Match the urgency to the business impact.

What's Next in This Series

  • Part 6: Production Agentic SAP Systems — monitoring, observability, cost management, failure handling, and scaling agentic workloads at enterprise SAP scale

The patterns in this article — interrupt(), confidence routing, smart escalation, timeout handling, and audit trail — are the production readiness layer that separates agentic SAP proofs of concept from systems organizations can actually trust and deploy. If you implement any of these patterns and find edge cases worth documenting, share them in the comments.

entry.title: Super Admin Access
entry.content:
In our environment, Super Admin access is currently only assigned to background technical users (e.g. SFADMIN, IPSADMIN). For our transactional (IT) administrators, we use a dedicated permission group to provide access to manage and configure the system, including Manage Provisioning Access and the ability to grant Learning Administration access. This level of access is restricted to a small number of IT administrators.

With the latest release, the Manage Super Admin Access functionality has changed. As a result, our IT administrators are now classified as Admin Overseers, while our background technical users remain Super Admins. This change has prompted us to reassess our approach and raises the following questions:

  • Should background technical users be set-up as Super Admins?
  • Should our IT administrators be defined as Super Admins rather than Admin Overseers?
  • What is the rationale for including Provisioning and Learning Administration access as part of Super Admin access, given that additional permissions are still required to manage most functional areas of the system?

We’d be interested to understand how others are interpreting this change and what best practice looks like following the enhancement.

entry.title: Enhance Your AI Knowledge in SAP Finance: New Learning Journey Now Available
entry.content:
The future of finance is here, and it's powered by artificial intelligence. Are you ready to lead the charge in AI-driven financial transformation? We're excited to announce the launch of our latest learning journey: Applying Business AI Solutions and Expertise in SAP Finance

Why This Learning Journey Matters

In today's rapidly evolving business landscape, finance professionals who can harness the power of AI aren't just staying competitive; they're becoming indispensable. This intermediate learning journey is designed specifically for the next generation of finance professionals who are ready to take the lead in digital transformation and unlock the full potential of SAP's cutting-edge AI solutions.

What You'll Learn

This learning journey takes you on an immersive exploration of key AI tools that are revolutionizing finance operations:

  • SAP Joule: Discover how this intelligent assistant transforms everyday finance tasks
  • SAP Joule Agents: Learn to leverage specialized AI agents for complex financial processes
  • SAP Joule for Consultants: Master the tools that empower finance consulting excellence
  • SAP Business Data Cloud in Finance: Unlock the potential of intelligent data management

A Hands-On Learning Experience

What sets this learning journey apart is its practical, experience-driven approach. You explore Joule features in Finance firsthand through practical, hands-on exercises that enable learners to experience these capabilities themselves. You'll follow a fictitious consultant's journey as they implement AI tools across various finance business processes, giving you real-world context and actionable insights you can apply immediately.

Starting Your AI Journey? We've Got You Covered

If you're new to AI in finance, we recommend starting with our beginner-level learning journey: Discovering SAP Business AI Capabilities for SAP Finance. This foundational course introduces you to the core concepts and capabilities of AI in the finance domain, providing the perfect stepping stone before diving into the more advanced, hands-on content in our new intermediate learning journey. Whether you're taking your first steps or ready to deepen your expertise, we have a path designed for your learning needs.

Curious?

The intersection of AI and finance represents one of the most exciting opportunities in today's business world. By completing our learning journey, you'll not only gain technical expertise but also position yourself as an expert in the AI-driven future of finance. Start learning right now!

entry.title: 📞 Last Call - Financials Forum 2026 📞
entry.content:
In two weeks the Financials Forum 2026 starts! Secure your spot now for the hybrid event from 27–29 April 2026 in Walldorf or online.

Please note: The event language is German.  

📌What to expect in just a few weeks:
• 18 training presentations on Finance, Controlling, Central Finance & more - each with in-depth knowledge, live demos and Q&A
• 3 basic hands-on sessions - participate directly in the SAP live system and try things out yourself
• 2 meet-the-expert sessions - an exclusive opportunity to ask your individual questions to SAP specialists
• 1 customer presentation - authentic insights from successful SAP S/4HANA Finance projects in practice
• On-site supporting program: networking evening and tours of the CFO Lab, the Experience Center and the S.Mart Store

📝Plan your personal agenda
Several parallel tracks let you flexibly combine the topics most important to you. Whether finance processes, innovations or system hands-on - choose exactly what will help you move forward.

🔗Agenda: https://www.sap.com/germany/documents/2026/01/8ea788a5-3a7f-0010-bca6-c68f7e60039b.html
🔗Registration: https://training.sap.com/course/wdefit-sap-financials-forum-classroom-026-de-en?

Have questions? Feel free to write to us!

We look forward to seeing you!
Alina Macarie, Lena Eigendorf & Janina Igers
